---
title: "Why This Matters"
format:
  html:
    html-math-method: mathml
    toc: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

Mobile-phone-derived mobility data can provide timely evidence on population
movement, commuting, displacement, transport demand, and service catchments. The
same data are also selective. People, places, devices, operators, and observation
windows are not represented evenly.

## The Challenge

An observed device count is not a population count. An observed OD flow is not a
neutral measurement of the true movement system. If mobile phone data under-cover
some places and over-cover others, then the observed OD matrix can reproduce
those inequalities as if they were mobility patterns.

In notation, the observed flow $F^{mpd}_{ij}$ can differ from the target flow
$F^{bench}_{ij}$ or the unobserved true flow because coverage varies across
origins, destinations, and social contexts. Area $i$ may have population
$P_i$, observed active users $U_i$, coverage score $p_i = U_i / P_i$, and
coverage bias $b_i = 1 - p_i$.

## Consequences

If these biases are ignored, downstream estimates can mislead decisions:

- displacement analysis can miss groups with low digital coverage
- transport models can overstate movement from highly observed areas
- public health exposure estimates can inherit spatial coverage bias
- company data products can be used outside the populations they represent
- policy comparisons can confuse data availability with real mobility

The problem is not that mobile-phone-derived data are unusable. The problem is
that their measurement process must be made visible, adjusted where possible,
and validated against external evidence.

## What `debiasR` Adds

`debiasR` supports a practical workflow:

- measure coverage and representativeness bias
- model how bias varies across places
- adjust OD flows using a menu of methods with different data requirements,
  assumptions, and levels of model complexity
- validate adjusted flows against benchmark OD data

The package's main innovation is `adjust_multilevel_bayes()`. It treats observed
OD flows as biased counts and estimates adjusted flows through a multilevel count
model that can include origin and destination attributes, distance, coverage
residuals, and hierarchical structure. The other adjustment methods remain
important because they are useful options in their own right: some require only
coverage inputs, some use benchmark margins, and some directly calibrate against
benchmark OD flows. Method choice should therefore depend on the available data,
the target use case, and the validation evidence.

## What Adjustment Enables

After adjustment, users can compare raw and adjusted OD matrices, inspect which
places changed most, and assess whether the adjusted data better reproduce
benchmark flows. Adjustment does not remove the need for judgement. It makes the
assumptions explicit enough to test, discuss, and report.
